title: Slide sweeps boldly to lake
summary: Slide sweeps boldly to lake Eklutna avalanche blocks trail, turns 120 acres into toothpicks By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Reporter While attention was focused on avalanches burying highways last week, a monster slide was preparing to let loose in the high country above Eklutna Lake. Whether it was triggered by a blast of wind or a few final, critical snowflakes, no one knows. No one saw a slide break off from a snow-laden ridge near the top of 7,500-foot Bold Peak and rapidly gain speed. No one heard it rumble down the mountain in an astonishing display of power. As it tumbled, it gathered momentum and snow, rushing first through a gully then dropping into a pillowy bowl, where it blasted loose a snow slab 30 feet deep before plunging down the slope below. By the time it rolled to a stop,…

title: State school spending faces revised test
summary: State school spending faces revised test By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter Researchers for the newspaper Education Week recently gave Alaska decision makers a failing grade for spending so much more in rural school districts like the Southwest Region than in Anchorage. But if the state did what Education Week is urging, the rural districts - already behind academically, with worse buildings and less experienced teachers - would get less money. And Anchorage - which already has far higher standardized test results than most rural districts, the broadest curriculum, nicer facilities and more experienced teachers - would get more money. Education Week acknowledges its formula for judging the fairness of states' education spending has weaknesses and has proposed a different method for …

title: APU to enroll high school seniors
summary: APU to enroll high school seniors By LARRY CAMPBELL Daily News reporter Some high school seniors think they just can't wait. College, challenging classes, a new environment. They whet their appetites with a course or two at the local university. It works. They want more. For that kind of high school senior, Alaska Pacific University may have all you can handle - and a year early. In an agreement to begin next year, a select group of high school seniors will be able to attend APU classes on campus, get college credit and still get the last year's course work they need to earn diplomas from the Anchorage School District. In effect, students would be enrolled in the School District and APU at the same time. "The whole idea is to give (seniors) a taste of college while still technically in hig…

title: Plot twist has Stabenow fans hot
summary: Plot twist has Stabenow fans hot By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter "You are a murderer," the woman told mystery writer Dana Stabenow. "You have no conscience ... and you don't care about those of us who object to ... murder." Stabenow's fans are fuming. (If you plan to read her current book, "Hunter's Moon," but haven't yet, you might want to stop here to avoid learning what Stabenow did.) "I don't know if I can read this series anymore," wrote one reader. "I don't need heartbreak in television, movies, or fiction. I get enough of it from everyday real life." Stabenow's crime was to kill off a major character in her Kate Shugak mystery series. Yep, Kate's boyfriend, Jack, takes an unexpected dirt nap. At the end of "Hunter's Moon," Stabenow also left Kate's quirky wolf/dog, Mutt, seriou…

title: School bonds to face voters
summary: School bonds to face voters By TIM PRYOR Daily News reporter Chugiak and Eagle River students will have more room to breathe and learn if Anchorage voters approve bond proposals for nearly $120 million that were placed on the April 4 ballot Tuesday by the Anchorage Assembly. The Assembly approved the plan despite warnings that adding expensive projects in those communities to the bond package could doom the entire bond proposal. The plan, approved overwhelmingly by the Assembly, actually puts two separate proposals before city voters. The first bond plan, valued at about $77.9 million, includes spending for citywide improvements endorsed by the Anchorage School Board and $20.8 million in renovations at Chugiak High School. A second bond proposal of about $42 million would build an 800-stud…

title: Native rights activist ends hunger strike
summary: Native rights activist ends hunger strike The Associated Press JUNEAU - Native activist Desa Jacobsson has ended her hunger strike aimed at drawing attention to subsistence rights for urban Alaska Natives. Jacobsson, 51, and Alaska Federation of Natives President Julie Kitka announced the end of Jacobsson's fast late Friday. Kitka says the fight for subsistence rights will continue on a new battle ground: Washington, D.C. Kitka and Jacobsson have scheduled meetings with members of the Clinton administration this week, Kitka told the Juneau Empire. Jacobsson began her hunger strike Jan. 10 to draw attention to the subsistence rights of Alaska Natives who live in urban areas, where subsistence hunting and fishing are prohibited. Being cut off from their ancient tradition of subsistence fishi…

title: Chugiak changes its plans
summary: Chugiak changes its plans Region IV wrestling now personal matter By BETH BRAGG Daily News executive sports editor While Service High did the team thing - again - at the Region IV wrestling championships Friday, things got personal for Chugiak. The Mustangs, who hoped to battle Service High in the team standings this week at the region meet and next week at the state meet, had to switch game plans when they showed up for first-round action at Service. They entered the meet without Stan Brown, a 119-pounder who was among the region's top seeds before being declared ineligible this week. Without him, Chugiak turned its attention to individual glory rather than team triumphs. All of which seems to assure that Service, an unsurmountable force in Region IV for 26 straight years, will remain dom…

title: 'Flying through a graveyard'
summary: 'Flying through a graveyard' Wolves short of prey, hunters say in McGrath By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter McGRATH - One after another, in plaid shirts and snowmobile suits and bunny boots, they stepped up to the microphone in the center of the school gym to testify at a wolf summit here last week. Everyone agreed: The wolves in this part of Interior Alaska are multiplying, and that's bad. The state must start killing the predators and kill them soon, before they wipe out the moose that human hunters depend on. "We have to do something pretty quick or we are going to suffer - everybody," said Andrew Fredericks, a gray-haired elder from Sleetmute. McGrath is at the center of what is fast becoming the latest chapter in Alaska's long and angry debate over wolf killing. Supporters of wolf con…

title: Colorado College hits UAA where it hurts
summary: Colorado College hits UAA where it hurts By DOYLE WOODY Daily News assistant sports editor The Seawolves' second-night superiority went south Saturday. So, too, did their standing in the Western Collegiate Hockey Association. Colorado College humbled the University of Alaska Anchorage 4-0 at Sullivan Arena to hand the Seawolves a rare loss in the second game of a series and knock UAA from fourth to fifth in the WCHA standings. Defenseman Paul Manning scored two goals for the Tigers, who leapfrogged over the Seawolves and into fourth place. UAA slipped to 12-11-3 overall and 10-9-3 in the WCHA while Colorado College improved to 15-12-2 and 11-7-2. The Seawolves entered the game 8-3-0 in the second game of series this season, but came out stunningly flat and suffered for it. They earned just…

title: 'Lifestyle suicide' mission
summary: 'Lifestyle suicide' mission Doctor crusades to get Alaskans on their feet and moving By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News Reporter "I've been known to rant on this particular subject," Dr. Peter Mjos warned his audience, then did so. Alaskans are committing "lifestyle suicide," he said, and something has to be done right now to save our children from shortened lives riddled with disease and depression. Mjos, a family doctor at the Alaska Native Medical Center, is on a mission. He's writing newsletters and talking to any group that will listen about the dangerous decline of physical activity among Americans, including Alaskans. Only about half of Americans ages 12 to 21 are physically active on a regular basis, according to the Centers for Disease Control. And once kids hit high school, the numbers …

title: Teen hit by truck on icy road dies
summary: Teen hit by truck on icy road dies By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter On Saturday afternoon, Lohnnie Hibben struggled through tears as she talked about her 18-year-old son Christopher Naufahu, who was pronounced brain dead earlier that morning. A machine was keeping his organs alive so they could be used to save other people. It was only two days ago - two long days - that Naufahu's aunt's car skidded off the Seward Highway into a deep, slushy ditch, Hibben said Saturday. "The roads were really, really bad," she said. Naufahu's aunt abandoned the car and then returned with Naufahu, his uncle Wes Hibben, another uncle and a cousin in a truck, she said. "He was supposed to be at work," Lohnnie Hibben said. "But he just said he would be late and would see if he could help." "He was the most g…

title: Truck hits teen trying to move car
summary: Metro News - Truck hits teen trying to move car - Car stuck in Girdwood? Take the bus Truck hits teen trying to move car Top | Next An Anchorage teenager was trying to move a car that had become stuck on the side of a road Thursday night when a pickup truck hit him, Anchorage police said. Christopher Naufahu, 18, was listed in critical condition Friday at Providence Alaska Medical Center. Police said Naufahu was a passenger in a car that lost control on an icy roadway about 8:40 p.m. and became stuck on the side of the Seward Highway just north of DeArmoun Road. The driver of a pickup, Brandon Griffin, 21, saw the flashing lights and applied his brakes, police said. Griffin lost control, however, and his pickup skidded into Naufahu, pinning him between the two vehicles, according to police…

title: Hope expects long siege without power
summary: Hope expects long siege without power By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter Hope Postmaster Linda Graham dutifully reports to work every morning, but she hasn't been staying long the past few days. "I go over and check on it every morning, but there's no heat because there's no electricity," Graham said. No matter. There's no mail, either. Like other Kenai Peninsula towns shut off from the rest of the world for almost a week by a siege of avalanches and power outages, folks in Hope are learning to live in the 21st century by 19th century rules. No television. No newspapers. No water, in a lot of homes. Houses in Hope are on water wells, and wells need power for pumps. Some people have backup generators, but generators require fuel. As with most other staples of contemporary life, Hope is runni…

title: Slide kills railroad employee
summary: Slide kills railroad employee Bulldozer blasted into Inlet By RICHARD MAUER Daily News reporter Laboring in the most extreme avalanche conditions in decades, an Alaska Railroad worker assisting highway crews at a slide near Bird Flats on the Seward Highway suffered fatal injuries Tuesday when a second avalanche fell from the mountains and crushed him. Kerry Brookman, 53, a heavy equipment operator with the railroad for 21 years, died Tuesday evening after being airlifted to Providence Alaska Medical Center and spending most of the afternoon in surgery. He suffered a crushed pelvis, internal injuries and bleeding. Brookman, operating a bulldozer, was working with two other state highway bulldozer operators when the second avalanche struck about 12:30 p.m. Brookman's 35,000-pound D6 Caterpil…

title: Steller report faulted
summary: Steller report faulted Judge tells agency to do its job right By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Go do your job and do it right this time, a federal judge said this week in ordering the National Marine Fisheries Service to sort out whether commercial fish trawling is hurting the endangered Steller sea lion in the North Pacific. Until the agency does that, it is in violation of federal law, U.S. District Judge Thomas Zilly of Seattle wrote in a long opinion elaborating on his one-page order from last week. The NMFS had prepared a report evaluating how the fishing affects the sea lions. But Zilly found that review "limited in scope, heavy on general background information and deficient in focused and meaningful discussion and analysis." The review is "not the broad and in-depth consulta…

title: 'Weather goddess' takes control in Girdwood
summary: 'Weather goddess' takes control in Girdwood By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter GIRDWOOD - This resort town is used to snow. Just not this much. The snow has stacked up in town, piled up on the mountains. It has stranded travelers, stuck between avalanches on the Seward Highway to the north and south. And the people who live here are digging out their homes. "This is more snow than even we can handle,"Girdwood resident Steve Poirot said Tuesday. "I haven't seen my plow guys for a few days." Poirot, his four children and his wife, Tanya, spent Tuesday morning shoveling snow. They figure about four feet has fallen at their home since Thursday. "Even with your snowshoes, you sink in to your waist," Poirot said as he stood outside his Chevrolet Suburban at the Tesoro station where the Alyeska H…

title: Copyright © 2000 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Facing the music Hong Kong rock star dumped in Anchorage after ruckus on jet By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter A Hong Kong rock star caused a passenger jet to make an unplanned stop in Anchorage on Thursday after he started smoking and singing from his first-class seat, put a flight attendant in a headlock, assaulted passengers and lay on the floor screaming obscenities. Ronald Cheng, 27, was finally subdued when the pilot of the nonstop EVA Air Flight 05 from Los Angeles to Taipei, Taiwan, handed control of his aircraft to another crew member, stepped back into the cabin and ended up hitting Cheng in the head with a flashlight, according to an affidavit filed by the FBI in federal court. Cheng was charged with crimes aboard an aircraft, including interfering with flight crew members and …

title:
Regulators
rejected oil company merger
summary: Regulators rejected oil company merger By H. JOSEF HEBERT Associated Press Writer WASHINGTON (AP) � Worried about the impact on gasoline prices on the West Coast, the Federal Trade Commission today rejected the merger of BP Amoco and Atlantic Richfield Co., and told its staff to ask the courts to block the $30 billion deal. The commission voted 3-2 to seek a court injunction against the merger, troubled for months because of agency concern the new company would have too much control over Alaska oil production. ³We believe we have a compelling case," BP Amoco said in a statement, promising to "argue the case vigorously" in court. The company also said it was open to further negotiations. "We have been and remain willing to discuss any reasonable options that might led to a negotiated settle…
